title: Today's Tech Brief: Strava Tightens API Access
summary: Strava restricts API access, Computex 2026 kicks off
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Strava blames zero-code AI apps and scrapers as it tightens API access
imageCaption: Strava blames zero-code AI apps and scrapers as it tightens API access (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. Strava is making some changes to its API access. The fitness tracking giant is blaming zero-code AI apps and scrapers for the tightening of its API access. What does that mean? Well, basically, Strava is making it harder for developers to build apps using its data without paying a pretty penny - $11.99 a month, to be exact. I'm guessing some developers might be feeling a bit frustrated about this, but it's understandable that Strava wants to protect its data.

This move seems like a way for Strava to regain control over its data. It seems like a lot of developers were using Strava's API to build apps without really contributing back to the platform. Now, Strava wants a slice of the action. It's a bit like a club deciding to charge a membership fee to hang out - you can't just crash the party anymore. I'm curious to see how this will affect the types of apps that get built on top of Strava's data.

Meanwhile, Computex 2026 is kicking off in Taipei, Taiwan. This is one of the biggest tech conferences in the world, and it's a chance for major players like Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, and Intel to show off their latest and greatest. My read is that this conference will likely bring some exciting announcements, especially in the areas of AI and computing. It's always fun to see what new gadgets and tech is on the horizon.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
